question_text: The ABO blood group system was discovered by:
explanation: The ABO blood group system was discovered by Karl Landsteiner in 1900. He observed that the sera of some individuals caused agglutination (clumping) of red blood cells of other individuals, leading him to classify human blood into three groups: A, B, and C (later renamed O). The fourth group AB was discovered by Decastello and Sturli in 1902. For this groundbreaking discovery, Karl Landsteiner was awarded the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine in 1930.

elimination_hints.hint: William Harvey is famous for discovering blood circulation, not blood groups.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing discovery of blood circulation with blood group discovery

elimination_hints.hint: Robert Koch is known for Koch's postulates and discovering causative agents of tuberculosis and anthrax.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing microbiologists with immunologists

elimination_hints.hint: Gregor Mendel is the father of genetics. He worked on inheritance patterns in pea plants, not blood groups.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing genetics pioneer with blood group discoverer

question_text: A person with blood group AB has:
explanation: In the ABO blood group system, blood group is determined by the presence of antigens on the surface of red blood cells and antibodies in the plasma. A person with blood group AB has both antigen A and antigen B on the surface of their RBCs but has no antibodies (neither anti-A nor anti-B) in their plasma. This is why AB individuals are called 'universal recipients' — they can receive blood from any ABO group without agglutination because they lack both antibodies.

options.text: Antigen A and antibody b in plasma
Antigen B and antibody a in plasma
Both antigens A and B, but no antibodies
No antigens but both antibodies a and b

elimination_hints.hint: Having antigen A and antibody b describes blood group A, not AB.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing blood group A with blood group AB

elimination_hints.hint: Having antigen B and antibody a describes blood group B, not AB.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing blood group B with blood group AB

elimination_hints.hint: No antigens but both antibodies describes blood group O, not AB. This is the exact opposite of AB.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing blood group O with blood group AB

question_text: A person with blood group O is considered a 'universal donor' because:
explanation: Blood group O individuals have neither antigen A nor antigen B on the surface of their RBCs. When their blood is transfused to any recipient (A, B, AB, or O), the recipient's antibodies have no antigens to react with on the donated RBCs, so no agglutination occurs. However, in practice, whole blood from group O cannot truly be given universally because group O plasma contains both anti-A and anti-B antibodies. In modern medicine, only packed RBCs (not whole blood) from O donors are used as 'universal', and Rh factor must also be considered.

options.text: Their RBCs have both antigens A and B
Their RBCs lack both antigens A and B
Their blood contains a special compatibility factor

elimination_hints.hint: Having both antigens A and B describes blood group AB (universal recipient), not group O.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing universal donor with universal recipient

elimination_hints.hint: Actually, group O plasma has BOTH anti-A and anti-B antibodies. The reason for universal donation is about the absence of antigens on RBCs.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing what makes blood safe to donate — it's about donor RBC antigens, not donor plasma antibodies

elimination_hints.hint: There is no special compatibility factor. Universal donation is due to the absence of A and B antigens on RBCs.
elimination_hints.misconception: Inventing a non-existent mechanism

question_text: The Rh factor in blood is named after:
explanation: The Rh factor (Rhesus factor) was discovered by Karl Landsteiner and Alexander Wiener in 1940. It is named after the Rhesus monkey (Macaca mulatta), which was used in the experiments that led to the discovery of this blood group antigen. The Rh antigen (specifically the D antigen, the most immunogenic) is present on the surface of RBCs in about 80% of the human population (Rh-positive). Those who lack it are Rh-negative.

elimination_hints.hint: Rhizobium is a nitrogen-fixing bacterium found in root nodules of legumes. It has nothing to do with blood groups.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the prefix 'Rh' across unrelated biological contexts

elimination_hints.hint: The Rhine River has no connection to blood group discovery. Rh stands for Rhesus, the monkey used in experiments.
elimination_hints.misconception: Guessing a geographical origin

elimination_hints.hint: While there is an Rh protein, the name Rh was given because the antigen was first discovered using Rhesus monkey blood.
elimination_hints.misconception: Circular reasoning — the protein is named after the monkey, not vice versa

question_text: The ABO blood group in humans is an example of:
explanation: The ABO blood group is controlled by a single gene (gene I) with three alleles: Iᴬ, Iᴮ, and i. Alleles Iᴬ and Iᴮ are codominant with each other (both are expressed equally when present together, producing blood group AB), while both Iᴬ and Iᴮ are completely dominant over i (recessive). This is an example of multiple allelism (more than two alleles for a single gene) and codominance. The six possible genotypes (IᴬIᴬ, IᴬIᴮ, Iᴬi, IᴮIᴮ, Iᴮi, ii) produce four phenotypes (A, AB, B, O).

elimination_hints.hint: In incomplete dominance, the heterozygote shows an intermediate phenotype (e.g., pink flowers). In AB blood group, both A and B are fully expressed, not blended — this is codominance.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing codominance with incomplete dominance

elimination_hints.hint: Simple Mendelian dominance involves two alleles with one dominant. ABO has three alleles (Iᴬ, Iᴮ, i), making it more complex.
elimination_hints.misconception: Oversimplifying ABO genetics

elimination_hints.hint: Polygenic inheritance involves multiple genes controlling one trait (e.g., skin colour). ABO is controlled by a single gene with three alleles.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing multiple alleles with multiple genes

question_text: Erythroblastosis foetalis is caused by:
explanation: Erythroblastosis foetalis (Haemolytic Disease of the Newborn) occurs when an Rh-negative mother carries an Rh-positive foetus (the father being Rh-positive). During the first pregnancy, foetal Rh-positive RBCs may enter the maternal circulation (especially during delivery), sensitising the mother's immune system to produce anti-Rh antibodies (IgG). In subsequent pregnancies with Rh-positive foetuses, these maternal antibodies can cross the placenta and destroy the foetal RBCs, causing haemolysis, jaundice, anaemia, and potentially death. This can be prevented by administering anti-D immunoglobulin (RhoGAM) to the mother after the first delivery.

options.text: ABO incompatibility between mother and foetus
Rh incompatibility between an Rh-negative mother and Rh-positive foetus
Rh incompatibility between an Rh-positive mother and Rh-negative foetus
Deficiency of clotting factors in the foetus

elimination_hints.hint: While ABO incompatibility can cause mild haemolysis, classical erythroblastosis foetalis is specifically caused by Rh incompatibility.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing ABO incompatibility with Rh incompatibility

elimination_hints.hint: If the mother is Rh-positive, she already has the Rh antigen and will not produce anti-Rh antibodies. The problem arises only when the mother is Rh-NEGATIVE.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing which parent must be Rh-negative

elimination_hints.hint: Clotting factor deficiency causes haemophilia, not erythroblastosis foetalis.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing haemolytic disease with a clotting disorder

question_text: If a mother has blood group O (genotype ii) and the father has blood group AB (genotype IᴬIᴮ), what blood groups are possible in their children?
explanation: Mother's genotype: ii → she can only contribute allele i. Father's genotype: IᴬIᴮ → he can contribute either Iᴬ or Iᴮ. Possible offspring genotypes: Iᴬi (blood group A) or Iᴮi (blood group B). Therefore, all children will be either blood group A or blood group B. None of the children can have blood group O (would need ii from both parents) or blood group AB (would need Iᴬ and Iᴮ, but the mother can only provide i).

elimination_hints.hint: Blood group O requires genotype ii. Since the father is IᴬIᴮ, he cannot contribute an i allele — so O is not possible. AB requires IᴬIᴮ, but the mother can only give i.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not applying basic genetic cross logic

elimination_hints.hint: AB genotype (IᴬIᴮ) requires one Iᴬ and one Iᴮ allele. The mother (ii) cannot provide either Iᴬ or Iᴮ, so AB children are not possible.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking AB parents always produce AB children

elimination_hints.hint: Blood group O requires genotype ii. The father (IᴬIᴮ) always contributes either Iᴬ or Iᴮ, never i. So no child can be group O.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not recognising that group O needs i from both parents

question_text: During a mismatched blood transfusion, agglutination occurs because:
explanation: Agglutination is the clumping of red blood cells when incompatible blood is mixed. It occurs because antibodies in the recipient's plasma bind to the corresponding antigens on the donor's RBCs. For example, if a person with blood group A (who has anti-B antibodies) receives blood from a group B donor (who has B antigens on RBCs), the anti-B antibodies will bind to the B antigens, causing the RBCs to clump together (agglutinate). This agglutination blocks blood vessels and the clumped RBCs are destroyed, releasing haemoglobin which can damage kidneys and potentially cause death.

options.text: Antibodies in the recipient's plasma react with antigens on the donor's RBCs
Antigens in the recipient's plasma react with antibodies on the donor's RBCs
Platelets from both donor and recipient interact
White blood cells from the donor attack recipient's tissues

elimination_hints.hint: Antigens are on the RBC surface, not free in plasma. Antibodies are in plasma, not on RBCs. This option reverses the locations.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the locations of antigens and antibodies

elimination_hints.hint: Agglutination involves RBC antigens and plasma antibodies, not platelets.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing platelet aggregation with RBC agglutination

elimination_hints.hint: This describes a graft-versus-host reaction, not agglutination. Blood transfusion reactions involve antibody-antigen reactions on RBCs.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing transfusion reaction with immune rejection

question_text: Which of the following blood group genotypes will produce blood group A phenotype?
explanation: Blood group A can result from two possible genotypes: IᴬIᴬ (homozygous) or Iᴬi (heterozygous). In both cases, the Iᴬ allele produces antigen A on the RBC surface. In the heterozygous condition (Iᴬi), Iᴬ is dominant over i (recessive), so only antigen A is expressed. Genotype Iᴮi produces blood group B, and genotype IᴬIᴮ produces blood group AB (due to codominance).

elimination_hints.hint: IᴬIᴮ produces blood group AB due to codominance of both alleles, not blood group A.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking Iᴬ is dominant over Iᴮ

elimination_hints.hint: Iᴮi produces blood group B, since Iᴮ is dominant over i.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing Iᴬ and Iᴮ alleles

elimination_hints.hint: ii produces blood group O, as no antigens are produced on the RBC surface.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing blood group O genotype with blood group A

question_text: In a paternity dispute, a child has blood group O. The mother has blood group A. Which of the following men CANNOT be the father?
explanation: Child with blood group O has genotype ii. The mother with blood group A has genotype IᴬIᴬ or Iᴬi. Since the child must receive one i allele from each parent, the mother must be Iᴬi (heterozygous). The father must also contribute an i allele. A man with blood group AB (genotype IᴬIᴮ) cannot contribute an i allele — he can only give Iᴬ or Iᴮ. Therefore, a man with blood group AB cannot be the father. A man with blood group A (Iᴬi), B (Iᴮi), or O (ii) could potentially contribute an i allele.

elimination_hints.hint: A man with blood group A could have genotype Iᴬi and could contribute an i allele to produce an O child.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not considering that blood group A can be heterozygous (Iᴬi)

elimination_hints.hint: A man with blood group B could have genotype Iᴮi and could contribute an i allele.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not considering that blood group B can be heterozygous (Iᴮi)

elimination_hints.hint: A man with blood group O has genotype ii and would always contribute an i allele, making him a potential father.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly excluding group O

question_text: Which of the following statements about erythroblastosis foetalis is INCORRECT?
explanation: The incorrect statement is that erythroblastosis foetalis typically occurs during the first pregnancy. In reality, the first pregnancy with an Rh-positive foetus usually proceeds normally because the mother has not yet been sensitised. Sensitisation occurs when foetal Rh-positive blood enters the maternal circulation during delivery. The mother then produces anti-Rh antibodies (initially IgM, which cannot cross the placenta). In subsequent pregnancies, these antibodies have class-switched to IgG, which can cross the placenta and attack foetal Rh-positive RBCs. This is why the disease typically manifests from the second pregnancy onwards.

options.text: It is caused by Rh incompatibility between mother and foetus
It can be prevented by administering anti-Rh antibodies to the mother after delivery
It typically occurs during the first pregnancy itself
The maternal antibodies cross the placenta and destroy foetal RBCs

elimination_hints.hint: This is a correct statement. Erythroblastosis foetalis IS caused by Rh incompatibility. You need to find the INCORRECT statement.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not reading the question carefully — looking for the incorrect option

elimination_hints.hint: This is correct. Anti-D immunoglobulin (RhoGAM) given after the first delivery prevents sensitisation by destroying foetal Rh+ cells in maternal blood before the immune system reacts.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing about Rh prophylaxis

elimination_hints.hint: This is correct. Maternal IgG anti-Rh antibodies cross the placenta and cause haemolysis of foetal Rh-positive RBCs.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing IgM (cannot cross placenta) with IgG (can cross placenta)

question_text: In a cross between a heterozygous blood group A (Iᴬi) Rh-positive (Rr) father and a blood group O (ii) Rh-negative (rr) mother, what is the probability that a child will be blood group A and Rh-positive?
explanation: This is a dihybrid cross: Father: Iᴬi × Rr → gametes: IᴬR, Iᴬr, iR, ir. Mother: ii × rr → gametes: ir only. Possible offspring: IᴬiRr (A, Rh+), Iᴬirr (A, Rh-), iiRr (O, Rh+), iirr (O, Rh-). Each has a 1/4 probability. Therefore, the probability of a child being blood group A AND Rh-positive (IᴬiRr) is 1/4 or 25%.

elimination_hints.hint: 1/2 would be the probability of getting either blood group A alone (Iᴬi vs ii → 1/2 chance of Iᴬi) or Rh+ alone (Rr vs rr → 1/2 chance of Rr). The combined probability is 1/2 × 1/2 = 1/4.
elimination_hints.misconception: Forgetting to multiply independent probabilities

elimination_hints.hint: 3/4 would apply if both parents were heterozygous for Rh (Rr × Rr). Here the mother is rr, so only 1/2 of children are Rh+.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using wrong parental genotypes for the Rh cross

elimination_hints.hint: 1/8 would apply if there were three independent traits. With two independent traits, each having 1/2 probability, the answer is 1/2 × 1/2 = 1/4.
elimination_hints.misconception: Miscalculating the combined probability

question_text:
Assertion (A): A person with blood group O has both anti-A and anti-B antibodies in their plasma.
Reason (R): Blood group O individuals lack both antigen A and antigen B on their red blood cells.
explanation: Both statements are true, and the Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion. In the ABO system, individuals produce antibodies against the antigens they lack. Since blood group O individuals (genotype ii) do not have either A or B antigens on their RBCs, their immune system produces both anti-A and anti-B antibodies (also called isoagglutinins) in their plasma. This is why group O blood can be dangerous to recipients if whole blood (containing plasma with antibodies) is transfused, but safe if only packed RBCs (lacking antigens) are given.

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: The Reason directly explains the Assertion. Antibodies are produced against antigens that are absent — so lacking both antigens leads to producing both antibodies.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not recognising the direct causal relationship between antigen absence and antibody production

elimination_hints.hint: The Reason is true. Blood group O (genotype ii) indeed lacks both A and B antigens on RBCs.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly rejecting a well-established fact

elimination_hints.hint: The Assertion is true. Blood group O individuals do have both anti-A and anti-B antibodies.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing blood group O antibody profile

question_text:
Assertion (A): Blood group AB individuals are called 'universal recipients'.
Reason (R): Blood group AB individuals have both Iᴬ and Iᴮ alleles, which are codominant.
explanation: Both the Assertion and the Reason are true, but the Reason is NOT the correct explanation of the Assertion. AB individuals are universal recipients because they lack both anti-A and anti-B antibodies in their plasma, so they can receive RBCs from any ABO group without agglutination. The fact that Iᴬ and Iᴮ are codominant explains why both antigens are present on RBCs (genetics), but the universal recipient property is due to the absence of antibodies (immunology), not the codominance of alleles.

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: Codominance explains why both antigens are present, not why AB individuals can receive all blood types. The correct reason is the absence of anti-A and anti-B antibodies.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing the genetic basis of antigen expression with the immunological basis of transfusion compatibility

elimination_hints.hint: The Reason is true — Iᴬ and Iᴮ alleles are indeed codominant, meaning both are expressed when present together.
elimination_hints.misconception: Incorrectly rejecting the codominance statement

elimination_hints.hint: The Assertion is true — AB individuals are indeed considered universal recipients in the ABO system.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not knowing that AB is the universal recipient group

question_text:
Assertion (A): Anti-Rh antibodies are not naturally present in Rh-negative individuals.
Reason (R): Anti-Rh antibodies are produced only after exposure to Rh-positive blood through transfusion or pregnancy.
explanation: Both statements are true, and the Reason correctly explains the Assertion. Unlike the ABO system where antibodies (anti-A, anti-B) develop naturally without prior exposure (due to cross-reaction with environmental antigens from bacteria and food), the Rh system requires actual exposure to the Rh antigen for antibody production. An Rh-negative person does not have anti-Rh antibodies unless they have been previously exposed to Rh-positive blood through a mismatched transfusion or pregnancy with an Rh-positive foetus. This is why the first Rh-incompatible pregnancy usually doesn't cause erythroblastosis foetalis.

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: The Reason directly explains WHY anti-Rh antibodies are absent — because they only form after exposure. This is a direct causal relationship.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not seeing the causal link between exposure requirement and natural absence

elimination_hints.hint: The Reason is true. Anti-Rh antibodies are indeed produced only after exposure to Rh-positive blood.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking Rh antibodies form naturally like ABO antibodies

elimination_hints.hint: The Assertion is true. Unlike ABO antibodies, anti-Rh antibodies are NOT naturally present in Rh-negative individuals.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assuming all blood group antibodies are naturally present

question_text:
Match the blood group in Column I with the correct antigen-antibody combination in Column II:

Column I:
(a) Blood Group A
(b) Blood Group B
(c) Blood Group AB
(d) Blood Group O

Column II:
(i) Both antigens A and B; no antibodies
(ii) Antigen A; antibody b (anti-B)
(iii) No antigens; both antibodies a (anti-A) and b (anti-B)
(iv) Antigen B; antibody a (anti-A)
explanation: (a)→(ii): Blood group A has antigen A on RBCs and anti-B antibodies in plasma. (b)→(iv): Blood group B has antigen B on RBCs and anti-A antibodies in plasma. (c)→(i): Blood group AB has both antigens A and B on RBCs but no antibodies in plasma (universal recipient). (d)→(iii): Blood group O has no antigens on RBCs but both anti-A and anti-B antibodies in plasma (universal donor). The key principle is that individuals produce antibodies against the antigens they lack.

elimination_hints.hint: Blood group A has antigen A (not both antigens), and blood group B has antigen B (not no antigens with both antibodies).
elimination_hints.misconception: Completely mixing up blood group characteristics

elimination_hints.hint: This swaps A and B groups. Blood group A has antigen A (not antigen B), and blood group B has antigen B (not antigen A).
elimination_hints.misconception: Swapping the antigen profiles of groups A and B

elimination_hints.hint: Blood group A has antigen A with anti-B (ii), not no antigens with both antibodies (iii). That description matches blood group O.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing blood group A with blood group O

question_text:
Match the genotype in Column I with the corresponding blood group phenotype in Column II:

Column I:
(a) IᴬIᴮ
(b) Iᴬi
(c) IᴮIᴮ
(d) ii

Column II:
(i) Blood Group O
(ii) Blood Group A
(iii) Blood Group AB
(iv) Blood Group B
explanation: (a)→(iii): IᴬIᴮ genotype produces blood group AB because Iᴬ and Iᴮ are codominant, so both antigens are expressed on the RBC surface. (b)→(ii): Iᴬi produces blood group A because Iᴬ is dominant over i, so only antigen A is expressed. (c)→(iv): IᴮIᴮ produces blood group B as both alleles code for antigen B (homozygous). (d)→(i): ii produces blood group O because no dominant allele is present to produce either antigen A or B.

elimination_hints.hint: IᴬIᴮ produces AB (iii), not A (ii). Since Iᴬ and Iᴮ are codominant, both antigens are expressed.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not understanding codominance — thinking one allele dominates the other

elimination_hints.hint: IᴬIᴮ produces AB (iii), not B (iv). And Iᴬi produces A (ii), not O (i).
elimination_hints.misconception: Misassigning genotypes to phenotypes

elimination_hints.hint: IᴬIᴮ produces AB (iii), not O (i). O requires the ii genotype with no dominant alleles.
elimination_hints.misconception: Completely confusing the genotype-phenotype relationship

question_text: Statement: Approximately 80% of the human population is Rh-positive.
explanation: This statement is TRUE. In the human population, approximately 80% (in some populations up to 85%) of individuals are Rh-positive, meaning they have the Rh antigen (specifically the D antigen) on the surface of their red blood cells. The remaining ~20% who lack this antigen are Rh-negative. The prevalence varies among different populations — it is about 85% in Caucasians, over 95% in Asian and African populations. The Rh-positive trait is dominant (Rh+ can be RR or Rr) while Rh-negative is recessive (rr).

elimination_hints.hint: The statement is factually correct. Approximately 80% of humans are Rh-positive. This is a standard NCERT fact.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not remembering the Rh-positive prevalence percentage

question_text: The substance administered to an Rh-negative mother after her first delivery to prevent erythroblastosis foetalis in subsequent pregnancies is called ________.
explanation: Anti-Rh antibodies (also known as anti-D immunoglobulin or Rh immune globulin, commercially known as RhoGAM) are administered to an Rh-negative mother within 72 hours of delivering an Rh-positive baby. These antibodies destroy any foetal Rh-positive red blood cells that may have entered the maternal circulation during delivery, before the mother's immune system can recognise them and mount an immune response. This prevents sensitisation of the mother and protects subsequent Rh-positive foetuses from erythroblastosis foetalis.

elimination_hints.hint: Anti-A antibodies are related to the ABO blood group system, not the Rh system. Erythroblastosis foetalis is caused by Rh incompatibility.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing ABO and Rh blood group systems

elimination_hints.hint: Erythropoietin (EPO) is a hormone that stimulates red blood cell production. It is not used for Rh prophylaxis.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing EPO (a haematopoietic hormone) with immunoglobulin therapy

elimination_hints.hint: Thrombopoietin stimulates platelet production. It has no role in preventing erythroblastosis foetalis.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing growth factors with immunoglobulins

question_text: In a hospital, a patient with blood group B and Rh-negative urgently needs a blood transfusion. The blood bank has the following units available: (1) A Rh-positive, (2) B Rh-negative, (3) O Rh-positive, (4) AB Rh-negative. Which unit should be given to the patient?
explanation: The patient is B Rh-negative. For a safe transfusion, the donor blood must be compatible in both ABO and Rh systems. The patient has anti-A antibodies (no anti-B) and is Rh-negative (should not receive Rh-positive blood to avoid Rh sensitisation). Unit 1 (A Rh+) is incompatible in both ABO and Rh. Unit 2 (B Rh-) is compatible in both systems — no ABO mismatch (B antigens won't be attacked by patient's antibodies since patient is also B) and Rh-compatible. Unit 3 (O Rh+) is ABO compatible (O has no antigens) but Rh-incompatible. Unit 4 (AB Rh-) is ABO incompatible because AB RBCs have A antigens that will be attacked by the patient's anti-A antibodies.

elimination_hints.hint: A Rh+ is incompatible in BOTH systems. The patient (B) has anti-A antibodies that would agglutinate A antigens, and Rh+ blood would sensitise an Rh- patient.
elimination_hints.misconception: Ignoring both ABO and Rh compatibility

elimination_hints.hint: While O blood has no ABO antigens (ABO-safe), the Rh-positive factor would sensitise the Rh-negative patient, risking future complications.
elimination_hints.misconception: Considering only ABO compatibility and ignoring Rh

elimination_hints.hint: AB blood has both A and B antigens. The patient's anti-A antibodies would agglutinate the A antigens on the donated AB RBCs.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking AB blood is universally compatible as a donor (confusing recipient and donor)
